skill.name: gif-sticker-maker
skill.description:
Convert photos (people, pets, objects, logos) into 4 animated GIF stickers with captions.
Use when: user wants to create cartoon stickers, GIF expressions, emoji packs, animated avatars,
or convert photos to Funko Pop / Pop Mart blind box style animations.
Triggers: sticker, GIF, cartoon, emoji, expression pack, avatar animation.
skill.summary: Convert photos into animated Funko Pop style GIF stickers using the MiniMax AI API.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: This skill converts photos into animated GIF stickers using the MiniMax AI API. Static analysis flagged 84 patterns, but most are false positives: argparse.ArgumentParser was misidentified as weak cryptography, and markdown bash code blocks were misidentified as Ruby backtick execution. Confirmed risks include legitimate subprocess calls to ffmpeg for video-to-GIF conversion, HTTP requests to documented MiniMax API endpoints, environment variable access for API keys, and local file read/write operations for image processing. All identified patterns are consistent with the skill documented purpose and pose no malicious intent.

high_findings.title: External command execution via ffmpeg subprocess
high_findings.description: The script scripts/convert_mp4_to_gif.py uses subprocess.run to invoke ffmpeg for MP4 to GIF conversion. Arguments are constructed from CLI input parameters (input file paths). While arguments are not shell-interpolated and use list form (preventing shell injection), user-controlled file paths are passed directly to the external process. This is a legitimate workflow requirement for the skill but represents an external command execution risk.

medium_findings.title: File read operations with user-provided paths
medium_findings.description: Scripts minimax_image.py and minimax_video.py read local image files via open() using paths derived from CLI arguments. The _encode_image function reads arbitrary files and encodes them as base64 data URIs for API submission. While legitimate for the skill purpose, this allows reading any file accessible to the process if a malicious actor controls CLI arguments.

medium_findings.title: File write operations to user-specified paths
medium_findings.description: Scripts write output files to paths specified via CLI arguments. The os.makedirs call with exist_ok=True creates directory structures as needed. While standard for a generation tool, this allows writing to any path the process has permission to access.

medium_findings.title: API key loaded from environment variable at module level
medium_findings.description: Both minimax_image.py and minimax_video.py read MINIMAX_API_KEY from environment at import time (module-level assignment). This means any script that imports these modules gains access to the API key value. The key is then included in HTTP Authorization headers for API requests.

low_findings.title: HTTP requests to external API endpoints
low_findings.description: Scripts make HTTP requests to MiniMax API endpoints (api.minimaxi.com and api.minimax.io). Base URLs are configurable via MINIMAX_API_BASE environment variable. All requests include timeouts and use HTTPS. This is expected behavior for an API client skill.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded API endpoint URLs in comments
low_findings.description: Default API base URLs are documented in comments at scripts/minimax_image.py and scripts/minimax_video.py. These are informational only and do not represent a security risk since the actual URL must be set via environment variable.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess execution with user-controlled input paths
dangerous_patterns.description: ffmpeg is invoked via subprocess.run with input file paths derived from CLI arguments. While the command uses list form (not shell=True), the input paths are not validated for existence beyond os.path.isfile checks. This pattern is expected for a media conversion skill but should be noted.

content.user_title: Create animated GIF stickers from photos
content.value_statement: Transform any photo into a set of four animated stickers with a distinctive Funko Pop 3D figurine style. This skill handles the complete workflow from image generation through animation to final GIF output, requiring only a source photo and a MiniMax API key.
actual_capabilities: Analyze uploaded photos to identify subject type (person, animal, object, or logo)
Generate four static sticker images in Funko Pop 3D figurine style via MiniMax Image API
Animate each static image into a short video via MiniMax Video API
Convert generated MP4 videos to animated GIF format using ffmpeg
Support facial likeness preservation for person subjects using subject reference
Provide multilingual caption support with language-consistent output
limitations: Requires a valid MiniMax API key and MINIMAX_API_BASE environment variable configured before use
Requires ffmpeg to be installed and available on the system PATH
Subject reference (facial likeness preservation) only works for person subjects, not animals or objects
Generated GIF file sizes can be large; no built-in optimization for web delivery
API rate limits and generation quotas apply based on your MiniMax account tier

use_cases.title: Social media sticker packs
use_cases.description: Create custom animated sticker packs from personal photos or brand mascots for messaging platforms and social media profiles.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators and social media managers

use_cases.title: Brand avatar animation
use_cases.description: Convert company logos or brand characters into consistent sets of animated expressions for marketing campaigns and internal communications.

use_cases.title: Personalized gifts and reactions
use_cases.description: Turn family photos or pet pictures into fun animated stickers that can be shared in group chats or printed as novelty items.

prompt_templates.title: Basic photo to stickers
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert my photo into 4 animated GIF stickers in Funko Pop style. Use default captions and actions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick sticker creation with default settings

prompt_templates.title: Custom caption stickers
prompt_templates.prompt: Create 4 animated stickers from this photo with custom captions: 'Hello', 'Haha', 'Oops', 'Love it'. Use Funko Pop 3D style with white background.
prompt_templates.scenario: Customized sticker pack with personalized text

prompt_templates.title: Pet sticker animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn this photo of my cat into 4 animated GIF stickers in Funko Pop style. The captions should be in Japanese. Preserve the cat distinctive markings in the generated images.

prompt_templates.title: Brand logo animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this company logo into 4 animated sticker expressions in 3D figurine style. Use English captions. Each sticker should show a different emotion: greeting, laughter, sadness, and affection. Output all as GIF files.
prompt_templates.scenario: Professional use case with specific emotional range

output_examples.input: A headshot photo of a smiling person
output: 4 GIF stickers generated (sticker_hi.gif, sticker_laugh.gif, sticker_cry.gif, sticker_love.gif)
Each sticker shows the person as a Funko Pop style 3D figurine with matching facial features
Captions displayed below each figurine in matching language
Soft studio lighting with white background on all outputs

output_examples.input: A photo of a dog sitting on grass
output: 4 animated GIF stickers with the dog rendered in cute 3D figurine style
Each sticker shows a different animated action (waving, laughing, crying, heart gesture)
Stylized rendering with Octane-quality lighting effects
GIF files ready for sharing in messaging apps

best_practices: Use clear, well-lit photos with the subject centered for best likeness preservation in generated stickers
Set MINIMAX_API_BASE to the correct regional endpoint (api.minimaxi.com for China, api.minimax.io for overseas) before running
Review generated GIF file sizes and adjust the ffmpeg width and fps parameters if outputs are too large for your use case
anti_patterns: Do not share your MiniMax API key in prompts or commit it to version control; always use environment variables
Do not use subject reference with non-person subjects (animals, objects) as the API will not preserve likeness
Do not mix languages between captions and the user interface language; keep all output in one consistent language

faq.question: What photo formats work best as input?
faq.answer: Clear JPEG or PNG photos work best. The subject should be well-lit and clearly visible. For person subjects, face-forward photos produce the most accurate likeness in the generated stickers.

faq.question: Do I need a MiniMax account to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. You need a MiniMax account with an active API key. Set MINIMAX_API_KEY and MINIMAX_API_BASE environment variables before running the skill.

faq.question: Can this skill animate objects or logos?
faq.answer: Yes, but without likeness preservation. The subject reference flag only works for person subjects. For objects and logos, the skill relies on text descriptions in the prompt to generate the figurine style.

faq.question: Why is ffmpeg required?
faq.answer: ffmpeg is used in the final step to convert the MP4 video files from MiniMax into animated GIF format. It performs a two-pass palette generation for better visual quality.

faq.question: How long does the full workflow take?
faq.answer: The workflow involves four image generation calls, four video generation calls (which are polled asynchronously), and four GIF conversions. Video generation typically takes the longest, often several minutes per video.

faq.question: Can I customize the sticker actions and animations?
faq.answer: Yes. You can provide custom captions and the skill will build matching animation prompts. The default actions are waving, laughing, crying, and heart gesture, but you can specify alternatives during the caption collection step.
